Lab 3: Mine Crafting¶

As with previous labs, be sure to submit only the lab3.ipynb and lab3.html version of the lab in your github Lab3 folder. No more, and no less.

Introduction¶

Imagine you are a scientist for a mining company that operates a vertical mine at the Earth's equator. This is one of the deepest mines on Earth; it's roughly 4 km to the bottom of the shaft. Your boss proposes to measure the vertical depth of the shaft by dropping a 1 kg test mass and accurately measuring the time to hit the bottom.

You will be guided in your investigations below and submit your report in the form of this notebook. Be sure to be quantitative in nature, produce highly polished plots, with appropriate titles, units, etc.

A table of relevant physical constants is below. Good luck!

Quantity Value
Gravitational Constant ($G$) $6.6743 \times 10^{-11}$ m$^3$/kg/s$^2$
Gravitational Acceleration ($g_o$) 9.81 m/s$^2$ (approximate)
Mass of the Earth ($M_{\oplus}$) $5.972 \times 10^{24}$ kg
Mass of the Moon ($M_{m}$) $7.35 \times 10^{22}$ kg
Radius of the Earth ($R_{\oplus}$) 6378.1 km
Radius of the Moon ($R_{m}$) 1738.1 km
Earth's Rotation Rate at the Equator $7.272 \times 10^{-5} $ rad/s
In [1]:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.integrate import solve_ivp
from scipy.integrate import quad
from scipy.fft import fft
from scipy.fft import fftfreq

from scipy import constants as cn
G = cn.G # m^2/kg/s^2
g = cn.g # m/s^2
M_e = 5.972e24 # kg
M_m = 7.35e22 # kg
R_e = 6378.1e3 # m
R_m = 1738.1e3 # m
Rot_e = 7.272e-5 # rad/s

Part 1: The Ideal Case¶

In this section and the following sections, we calculate the time for the 1 kg test mass to reach the bottom of the mineshaft under a series of increasingly complex assumptions. Remember that a projectile experiencing a constant gravitational force, plus a drag force, obeys the following second order differential equation:

$$ \frac{d^2y}{dt^2} = -g + \alpha \left| \frac{dy}{dt} \right|^{\gamma} \tag{1} $$

where $t$ is time, $y$ is the height, $g$ is the gravitational acceleration, $\alpha$ is the drag coefficient, and $\gamma$ is the speed dependence of the drag. For example, for $\gamma=2$, the drag force grows proportionally to the speed squared. Hint: pay particular attention to the sign conventions in your implementation! The drag, by definition, should oppose the direction of motion.

  1. First, make a simple calculation of how long a test mass would take to reach the bottom of the 4km shaft assuming no drag. This is the simple free-fall algebraic expression you know from PHYS 171.

  2. Next, reduce (1) into a system of coupled first order differential equations by setting the velocity $v = dy/dt$.

  3. Numerically solve (1) using solve_ivp assuming $\alpha=0$. Plot the position and velocity as a function of time.
    Draw a horizontal dashed line at the depth of the bottom of the shaft, to make it easy to see. Make the plot look nice, including displaying the position on the left axes, and plotting the velocity on a second right axis, using matplotlib twinx capability.
    In your notebook, label this figure clearly as Figure 1.

  4. Using the events detection capability of solve_ivp, calculate the time at which the mass hits the bottom of the shaft in the case of zero drag ($\alpha=0$). Compare this to your analytic calculation and discuss any differences you might see.

In [2]:
#part 1
x = 4000 #meters
#get analytical solution by rearranging kinematic equation x=gt^2
t_calc = (2*x/g)**0.5
print(f'Time to bottom: {t_calc:.3f}s')

Time to bottom: 28.562s

$$\frac{d^2y}{dt^2} = -g + \alpha \left| \frac{dy}{dt} \right|^{\gamma}$$ Using $v = dy/dt$ we can reduce to the system of the following two equations $$\frac{dy}{dt} = v$$ $$\frac{dv}{dt} = -g+ \alpha \left| v \right|^{\gamma}$$

In [3]:
#part 3
#define the equation to solve
def motion(t, init, g=g, gamma=2, a=0.):
    y, v = init
    dydt=v
    dvdt = -g + a*np.abs(v)**gamma
    return dydt, dvdt

#set up initial conditions
y0 = 0
v0 = 0
init0 = (y0, v0)
depth = -4000

#set up the time span based on calculated rresult above
t_span = (0, t_calc*1.5)
t_eval = np.linspace(0, t_calc*1.5, 1000)

soln = solve_ivp(motion, t_span, y0=init0, args=(g,2,0), t_eval=t_eval)
print(soln)


#plot the data
fig, ax1 = plt.subplots()
ax1.axhline(depth, linestyle='--', color='black', linewidth=0.5, label='Bottom (4000m)')

ax1.plot(soln.t, soln.y[0], label='Position (m)')
ax1.set_xlim(0, soln.t[-1])
ax1.set_ylim(depth*1.1, 0)
ax1.legend(loc='upper left')
ax1.set_xlabel('Time (s)')

ax2 = ax1.twinx()
ax2.set_ylabel('Velocity (m/s)')
ax2.plot(soln.t, soln.y[1], color='red', linewidth=2, label='Velocity')
ax2.legend(loc='upper right')

plt.title('Free fall plot without drag')

            [ 0.000e+00 -4.206e-01 ... -4.197e+02 -4.201e+02]]

In [4]:
#part 4
#define the event
def hit_bottom(t, init, g=g, gamma=2, alpha=0.):
    return init[0] - depth

#run the solver with the event
soln_event = solve_ivp(motion, t_span, init0, args=(g, 2, 0), events=hit_bottom, dense_output=True)
t_num = soln_event.t_events[0][0]
print(f"Time to bottom (numerical): {t_num:.3f} s")
print(f"Time to bottom (analytical): {t_calc:.3f} s")
if np.isclose(t_num, t_calc):
    print('The values are the same')
else:
    print('The values are not the same')

Time to bottom (numerical): 28.562 s
Time to bottom (analytical): 28.562 s
The values are the same

Part 2: Including Drag and a Variable g¶

In practice, we cannot ignore drag, and we should also not assume that $g$ is a constant. If you approximate that the mass of the Earth is distributed homogenously (which it is not!), then the gravitational constant $g$ will depend on your distance $r$ from the center of the Earth in a simple linear way:

$$ g(r) = g_o \left( \frac{r}{R_{\oplus}} \right) \tag{2} $$

where $g_o$ is the gravity at the surface, and $R_{\oplus}$ is the radius of the Earth.

  1. Make a new plot that shows the velocity and position as a function of
    time assuming $g(y)$. Be careful with coordinate systems! The test mass' position should be at the Earth radius at $t=0$, and falling down. But for plotting purposes, it will be useful to plot the height above the bottom of the shaft.
    In your notebook, label this figure clearly as Figure 2.

  2. What effect does incorporating a height-dependent $g$ have on the fall time? Explain.

  3. Now, turn on drag, and replot the position and velocity. For most things, $\gamma=2$ is a good assumption. How can you calibrate the value of $\alpha$? Think about what we did in Lecture 15/16, where we assumed a sky-diver's terminal speed was 50 m/s. You should assume the same of the test mass. What affect does including drag have on the fall time?

In [5]:
#part 1
#variable g (g decreases as you go deeper)
depth = -4000
def g_variable(y):
    r = R_e + y  # radius from Earth center
    return G * M_e / r**2
 
def motion_variable_g(t, init):
    y, v = init
    dydt = v
    g_local = g_variable(y)
    dvdt = -g_local
    return [dydt, dvdt]
 
#event function to detect hitting bottom
def hit_bottom(t, init, g=g, gamma=2, alpha=0.):
    return init[0] - depth
hit_bottom.terminal = True
hit_bottom.direction = -1

#set up initial conditions and time span
init0 = (0, 0)
t_span = (0, 35)
t_eval = np.linspace(0, 35, 1000)
 
#solve with variable g
sol_varg = solve_ivp(motion_variable_g, t_span, init0, t_eval=t_eval, events=hit_bottom, dense_output=True)

print(sol_varg.t[-1])
print(sol_varg.y[0][-1])
print(sol_varg.y[1][-1])
 
#plot the data - Figure 2
fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))

ax1.axhline(depth, linestyle='--', color='black', linewidth=0.5, label='Bottom (4000m)')
ax1.plot(sol_varg.t, sol_varg.y[0], label='Position (m)', color='blue')
ax1.set_xlim(0, sol_varg.t[-1])

ax1.set_ylim(depth*1.1, 0)
ax1.legend(loc='upper left')
ax1.set_xlabel('Time (s)')
ax1.set_ylabel('Position (m)')

ax2 = ax1.twinx()
ax2.set_ylabel('Velocity (m/s)', color='red')
ax2.plot(sol_varg.t, sol_varg.y[1], color='red', linewidth=2, label='Velocity')
ax2.legend(loc='upper right')

plt.title('Figure 2: Free fall with variable g(y)')

28.553553553553556
-3995.0654416991897
-279.8881050200652

In [6]:
#part 2
t_varg = sol_varg.t_events[0][0]
print(f"With variable g: Time to bottom = {t_varg:.3f} s")
    
#compare to constant g
def motion_constant_g(t, init):
    y, v = init
    return [v, -g]
 
sol_constg = solve_ivp(motion_constant_g, t_span, init0,
                       t_eval=t_eval, events=hit_bottom, dense_output=True)
t_constg = sol_constg.t_events[0][0]
print(f"With constant g: Time to bottom = {t_constg:.3f} s")
print(f"Difference: {t_varg - t_constg:.3f} s")
print(f"Variable g decreases slightly as you go deeper, reducing \nthe acceleration, so the fall time increases slightly.")

With constant g: Time to bottom = 28.562 s
Difference: 0.009 s
Variable g decreases slightly as you go deeper, reducing 
the acceleration, so the fall time increases slightly.
In [7]:
#part 3
#at terminal velocity: -g + alpha*v_term^gamma = 0
#so alpha = g / v_term^gamma
v_term = 50  # m/s (skydiver terminal velocity)
gamma = 2
alpha_calibrated = g / v_term**gamma
print(f"Calibrated alpha = {alpha_calibrated:.6f}")
print(f"terminal velocity = {v_term} m/s")
 
#define motion with drag
def motion_with_drag(t, init, g=g, gamma=2, alpha=0.):
    y, v = init
    dydt = v
    #drag opposes motion
    if v < 0:  # falling down
        dvdt = -g + alpha * np.abs(v)**gamma
    else:  # moving up
        dvdt = -g - alpha * np.abs(v)**gamma
    return [dydt, dvdt]
 
#solve with calibrated drag
sol_drag = solve_ivp(motion_with_drag, t_span, init0, args=(g, gamma, alpha_calibrated), t_eval=t_eval, events=hit_bottom, dense_output=True)
 
#plot comparison with different alphas
alphas = [0, 0.001, 0.01, alpha_calibrated]
fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))
 
for alpha in alphas:
    sol = solve_ivp(motion_with_drag, t_span, init0, args=(g, 2, alpha), t_eval=t_eval, events=hit_bottom, dense_output=True)
    
    ax1.plot(sol.t, sol.y[0], label=f'alpha={alpha:.4f}')
    ax2.plot(sol.t, sol.y[1], label=f'alpha={alpha:.4f}')
    
    if sol.t_events[0].size > 0:
        print(f"alpha={alpha:.4f}: Time to bottom = {sol.t_events[0][0]:.3f} s")
 
ax1.axhline(depth, linestyle='--', color='black', linewidth=0.5, label='Bottom')
ax1.set_xlabel('Time (s)')
ax1.set_ylabel('Position (m)')
ax1.set_title('Position vs Time')
ax1.legend()
 
ax2.set_xlabel('Time (s)')
ax2.set_ylabel('Velocity (m/s)')
ax2.set_title('Velocity vs Time')
ax2.legend()

print("The effect is drag significantly increases fall time and limits velocity.")

Calibrated alpha = 0.003923
terminal velocity = 50 m/s
alpha=0.0000: Time to bottom = 28.562 s
The effect is drag significantly increases fall time and limits velocity.

Part 3: The Coriolis Force¶

Since the Earth is rotating, there is a Coriolis force on the test mass as it falls. The force can be quite large. For example, imagine the mine shaft is on the Earth's equator and pointed towards the Earth's center. The equator rotates at about half a kilometer per second. So once the test mass has fallen for some time, it will bump into the wall, unless the shaft is very wide. The Coriolis force $\vec{F_c}$ is:

$$ \vec{F_c} = -2m \, \left( \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v} \right) \tag{3} $$

where $\vec{\Omega}$ is the Earth's rotation rate for a vector along $\hat{z}$ and $m$ is the mass of the object (1 kg for the test mass in this case). For a mine shaft on the equator, we might pick a right-handed coordinate system with the $\hat{x}$ axis along the East, $\hat{y}$ down into the mine shaft, and $\hat{z}$ along the North. This implies the component of the force are:

\begin{equation} F_{c_x} = + 2m \, \Omega v_y \tag{4} \end{equation} \begin{equation} F_{c_y} = - 2m \, \Omega v_x \tag{5} \end{equation} \begin{equation} F_{c_z} = 0 \tag{6} \end{equation}

  1. Extend and update your differential equations of motion to include the Coriolis force. You will need to add a Coriolis acceleration term to your equation for the depth coordinate (remember, $F=ma$), and additionally, start tracking the velocity and position in the transverse direction (``side-to-side'' in the shaft).

  2. Plot the transverse position of the object as a function of depth, initially assuming $\alpha=0$. That is, both axes will have units of distance. Plot ``dots" every few seconds so that you can see how the particle moves over time. Note that your depth and transverse axes are likely to have very different length scales. The depth will have a range of 4 km, while the transverse direction is about 5m.
    In your report, this should be Figure 3.

  3. If the mine shaft is 5m wide, and you drop the test mass from the center, does the test mass reach the bottom? Or does it bump into the wall first?

  4. Now turn drag back on. Does drag make any difference here?

In [8]:
#part 1
#coriolis force on equator with coordinates: x=East, y=down, z=North
def motion_coriolis(t, state):
    x, y, z, vx, vy, vz = state
    
    #coriolis acceleration components (Eq 4-6)
    ax_cor = 2 * Rot_e * vy
    ay_cor = -2 * Rot_e * vx
    az_cor = 0
    
    #total acceleration
    ax = ax_cor
    ay = -g + ay_cor
    az = az_cor
    
    return [vx, vy, vz, ax, ay, az]
 
def hit_bottom_3d(t, state):
    return state[1] - depth  # y coordinate is down
 
hit_bottom_3d.terminal = True
hit_bottom_3d.direction = -1
In [9]:
#part 2
print("\nPart 3.2: Plot transverse position vs depth - Figure 3")
print("-"*70)
 
#initial conditions: start at center, zero velocity
init_3d = [0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0]  # x, y, z, vx, vy, vz
t_span_3d = (0, 100)
t_eval_3d = np.linspace(0, 100, 1000)
 
sol_cor = solve_ivp(motion_coriolis, t_span_3d, init_3d, t_eval=t_eval_3d, events=hit_bottom_3d, dense_output=True)
 
#plot transverse position (x) vs depth (y) (Figure 3)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 10))
ax.plot(sol_cor.y[0], sol_cor.y[1], color='blue', linewidth=1.5)
 
#add dots every few seconds
dot_interval = 5  # seconds
dot_indices = [np.argmin(np.abs(sol_cor.t - t)) for t in np.arange(0, sol_cor.t[-1], dot_interval)]
ax.plot(sol_cor.y[0][dot_indices], sol_cor.y[1][dot_indices], 'ro', markersize=6)
 
ax.axhline(depth, linestyle='--', color='black', linewidth=0.5, label='Bottom')
ax.set_xlabel('Transverse Position (m) - Eastward')
ax.set_ylabel('Depth (m)')
ax.set_title('Figure 3: Transverse deflection due to Coriolis force')
ax.legend()

Part 3.2: Plot transverse position vs depth - Figure 3

#part 3
shaft_radius = 2.5  # m (5m diameter, so 2.5m radius from center)
 
if sol_cor.t_events[0].size > 0:
    t_impact = sol_cor.t_events[0][0]
    final_state = sol_cor.sol(t_impact)
    x_final = final_state[0]
    z_final = final_state[2]
    transverse_distance = np.sqrt(x_final**2 + z_final**2)
    
    print(f"Time to reach bottom: {t_impact:.3f} s")
    print(f"E deflection: {x_final:.3f} m")
    print(f"N deflection: {z_final:.6f} m")
    print(f"Total transverse distance from center: {transverse_distance:.3f} m")
    print(f"Shaft radius: {shaft_radius} m")
    
    if transverse_distance > shaft_radius:
        print("YES the mass hits the wall before reaching bottom")
    else:
        print("NO the mass reaches the bottom without hitting the wall")

Time to reach bottom: 28.562 s
E deflection: -5.539 m
N deflection: 0.000000 m
Total transverse distance from center: 5.539 m
Shaft radius: 2.5 m
YES the mass hits the wall before reaching bottom
In [11]:
#part 4
#define the function for having both forces
def motion_coriolis_drag(t, state, alpha=0.):
    x, y, z, vx, vy, vz = state
    
    #coriolis acceleration
    ax_cor = 2 * Rot_e * vy
    ay_cor = -2 * Rot_e * vx
    az_cor = 0
    
    #drag acceleration (opposes velocity)
    v_mag = np.sqrt(vx**2 + vy**2 + vz**2)
    if v_mag > 0:
        ax_drag = -alpha * vx * v_mag
        ay_drag = -alpha * vy * v_mag
        az_drag = -alpha * vz * v_mag
    else:
        ax_drag = ay_drag = az_drag = 0
    
    #total acceleration
    ax = ax_cor + ax_drag
    ay = -g + ay_cor + ay_drag
    az = az_cor + az_drag
    
    return [vx, vy, vz, ax, ay, az]
 
sol_cor_drag = solve_ivp(lambda t, s: motion_coriolis_drag(t, s, alpha_calibrated), t_span_3d, init_3d, t_eval=t_eval_3d, events=hit_bottom_3d, dense_output=True)
 
if sol_cor_drag.t_events[0].size > 0:
    t_drag = sol_cor_drag.t_events[0][0]
    x_drag = sol_cor_drag.sol(t_drag)[0]
    print(f"With drag: Time = {t_drag:.3f} s, Deflection = {x_drag:.3f} m")
    print(f"Without drag: Time = {t_impact:.3f} s, Deflection = {x_final:.3f} m")
    print("Drag reduces both fall time and deflection significantly.")

With drag: Time = 83.534 s, Deflection = -2.800 m
Without drag: Time = 28.562 s, Deflection = -5.539 m
Drag reduces both fall time and deflection significantly.

Part 4: An infinitely deep mine¶

Now, let us consider the theoretical case of an infinitely deep mine -- that is, a tunnel which traverses the full diameter of the Earth. For convenience, imagine the tunnel goes from pole-to-pole, such that $\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v} = 0$, and the Coriolis force can be neglected, as well as the drag force.

Additionally, continue assuming a constant density Earth.

  1. Again, plot the depth and velocity as a function of time. (Be careful with the meaning of $y$, as the particle can theoretically traverse fully through to the other side of the Earth.) Comment on what you see in the graph.
    In your notebook, label this figure clearly as Figure 4.

  2. How long will it take for the object to reach the other side? At what time does the object reach the center of the Earth, and at what speed?

  3. How does this ``crossing-time" compare to the orbital period, where you assume the object is on a circular orbit, in centripetal balance with the attractive force of the gravity. \begin{equation} \frac{v^2}{R} = \frac{GM}{R^2} \tag{7} \end{equation} gives the orbital speed $v$ for a given Earth radius $R$ and Earth mass $M$.

In [12]:
#part 1
#for infinite mine: g(r) = g0 * r/R_e (linear with radius)
def motion_infinite_mine(t, state):
    y, v = state
    r = R_e + y
    g_r = g * r / R_e
    dydt = v
    dvdt = -g_r
    return [dydt, dvdt]
 
#this creates shm so i can integrate for full period
T_analytical = 2 * np.pi * np.sqrt(R_e / g)
t_span_long = (0, T_analytical * 1.5)
t_eval_long = np.linspace(0, T_analytical * 1.5, 2000)
 
sol_inf = solve_ivp(motion_infinite_mine, t_span_long, init0,
                    t_eval=t_eval_long, dense_output=True)
 
#plot - Figure 4
fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
ax1.plot(sol_inf.t, sol_inf.y[0]/1000, label='Position', color='blue')
ax1.set_xlabel('Time (s)')
ax1.set_ylabel('Position (km)', color='blue')
ax1.legend(loc='upper left')
ax1.axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.5)
 
ax2 = ax1.twinx()
ax2.plot(sol_inf.t, sol_inf.y[1], color='red', linewidth=2, label='Velocity')
ax2.set_ylabel('Velocity (m/s)', color='red')
ax2.legend(loc='upper right')
ax2.axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.5)
 
plt.title('Figure 4: Oscillation in infinitely deep mine')

print("The mass oscillates sinusoidally in simple harmonic motion")

The mass oscillates sinusoidally in simple harmonic motion
In [13]:
#part 2 
#find when y = -R_e (center of Earth)
center_idx = np.argmin(np.abs(sol_inf.y[0] + R_e))
t_center = sol_inf.t[center_idx]
v_center = sol_inf.y[1][center_idx]
 
#find when y = -2*R_e (other side)
other_side_idx = np.argmin(np.abs(sol_inf.y[0] + 2*R_e))
t_other_side = sol_inf.t[other_side_idx]
 
print(f"Time to reach center: {t_center:.1f} s = {t_center/60:.1f} min")
print(f"Speed at center: {abs(v_center):.1f} m/s")
print(f"Time to reach other side: {t_other_side:.1f} s = {t_other_side/60:.1f} min")

In [14]:
#part 3
#orbital speed at Earth's surface: v = sqrt(GM/R)
v_orbital = np.sqrt(G * M_e / R_e)
T_orbital = 2 * np.pi * R_e / v_orbital
print(f"Crossing time (one way): {t_other_side:.1f} s = {t_other_side/60:.1f} min")
print(f"Orbital period: {T_orbital:.1f} s = {T_orbital/60:.1f} min")
print(f"Ratio (crossing/orbital): {t_other_side/T_orbital:.3f}")

Crossing time (one way): 2555.1 s = 42.6 min
Orbital period: 5069.4 s = 84.5 min
Ratio (crossing/orbital): 0.504

The ratio of $T_{Cross}$ to $T_{Orb}$ is very nearly $0.5$, meaning the crossing time equals one half of an orbital period.

Part 5: A non-uniform Earth¶

Finally, let us consider the case of a non-uniform Earth, and continue assuming we have no drag. We know from geology that the density increases towards the center. It rises from about 2-3 g/cm$^3$ near the surface, to more like 13 near the center. A simple model for the density $\rho$ as a function of distance from the center of the Earth $r$ is:

$$ \rho(r) = \rho_n \left( 1 - \frac{r^2}{R_{\oplus}^2} \right)^n \tag{8} $$

where $n$ is some exponent, and $\rho_n$ is a normalizing constant. The case of $n=0$ is the constant density Earth, while $n=2$ is closer to the real value. Note that the total mass of the Earth, $M$, must be conserved. Since the mass is the volume integral over the density:

\begin{equation} M = \int_V \rho(r) dV = \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} \int_0^{R_{\oplus}} \rho(r) r^2 \sin \phi \, dr \, d\phi \,d\theta = 4 \pi \int_0^{R_{\oplus}} \rho(r) r^2 \, dr \tag{9} \end{equation}

you will need to recompute the density constant $\rho_n$ for each case of $n>0$, You can use the quad integrator for this. Note that the analytic solution is straightforward to do by hand for $n=0$, and still manageable for $n=1,2$. You might consider doing that integral to make sure your method is right, and then you can be confident for $n>0$.

  1. Plot the normalized density profile (that is, set $\rho_n=1$) as a function of radius for $n=0, 1, 2, 9$.
    Put them all on the same plot, with a clear legend.
    In your notebook, label this figure clearly as Figure 5.

  2. Now, plot the force profile as a function of radius for $n=0, 1, 2, 9$.
    This step will show you if your normalization factor $\rho_n$ is correct, because the surface force must be the same for all $n$.
    In your notebook, label this figure clearly as Figure 6.

  3. Then, plot the position and velocity as a function of time for $n=0, 1, 2, 9$ as you did in Figure 1/2.
    In your notebook, label this figure clearly as Figure 7.

  4. For $n=0, 1, 2, 9$, compute the time to reach the center, and the speed achieved there.

Part 5, Section A (Density)¶

In [15]:
#define the funciton for normalized density
def rho_normalized(r, n):
    return (1 - (r/R_e)**2)**n
 
#plot for n = 0, 1, 2, 9
r_vals = np.linspace(0, R_e, 1000)
ns = [0, 1, 2, 9]
 
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
for n in ns:
    rho_vals = [rho_normalized(r, n) for r in r_vals] #mmmm fors within fors
    ax.plot(r_vals/1000, rho_vals, label=f'n={n}', linewidth=2)
 
ax.set_xlabel('Radius (km)')
ax.set_ylabel('Normalized Density ρ(r)/ρₙ')
ax.set_title('Figure 5: Density profiles for different n')
ax.legend()

#compute totla mass so that it = M_e
def compute_rho_n(n):
    def intg(r):
        return (1 - (r/R_e)**2)**n * r**2
    
    integral, _ = quad(intg, 0, R_e)
    rho_n = M_e / (4 * np.pi * integral)
    return rho_n
 
rho_n_values = {}
for n in ns:
    rho_n_values[n] = compute_rho_n(n)
    print(f"n={n}: ρ_n = {rho_n_values[n]:.2e} kg/m^3")

n=0: ρ_n = 5.49e+03 kg/m^3
n=1: ρ_n = 1.37e+04 kg/m^3
n=2: ρ_n = 2.40e+04 kg/m^3
n=9: ρ_n = 1.36e+05 kg/m^3

Part 5, Section B (Forces)¶

In [16]:
#define func for mass inside radius r
def mass_inside_r(r, n, rho_n):
    def integrand(r_prime):
        return (1 - (r_prime/R_e)**2)**n * r_prime**2
    
    integral, _ = quad(integrand, 0, r)
    return 4 * np.pi * rho_n * integral

#define func for force per unit mass at radius r
def force_at_r(r, n, rho_n):
    if r == 0:
        return 0
    M_r = mass_inside_r(r, n, rho_n)
    return G * M_r / r**2
 
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
for n in ns:
    force_vals = [force_at_r(r, n, rho_n_values[n]) for r in r_vals]
    ax.plot(r_vals/1000, force_vals, label=f'n={n}', linewidth=2)
 
ax.set_xlabel('Radius (km)')
ax.set_ylabel('g(r) (m/s²)')
ax.set_title('Figure 6: Force (g) profiles - all equal g at surface')
ax.legend()
print("All curves should reach g = 9.81 m/s^2 at surface (validates rho_n)")

All curves should reach g = 9.81 m/s^2 at surface (validates rho_n)

Part 5, Section C (Equations of Motion)¶

In [17]:
#part 3
def motion_nonuniform(t, state, n, rho_n):
    y, v = state
    r = R_e + y
    if r > 0:
        g_r = force_at_r(r, n, rho_n)
    else:
        g_r = 0
    dydt = v
    dvdt = -g_r
    return [dydt, dvdt]
 
#solve for each n
solutions_n = {}
fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))
 
for n in ns:
    sol = solve_ivp(lambda t, y: motion_nonuniform(t, y, n, rho_n_values[n]), t_span_long, init0, t_eval=t_eval_long, dense_output=True)
    solutions_n[n] = sol
    
    ax1.plot(sol.t, sol.y[0]/1000, label=f'n={n}', linewidth=2)
    ax2.plot(sol.t, sol.y[1], label=f'n={n}', linewidth=2)
 
ax1.set_xlabel('Time (s)')
ax1.set_ylabel('Position (km)')
ax1.set_title('Position vs Time')
ax1.legend()
ax1.axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.5)
 
ax2.set_xlabel('Time (s)')
ax2.set_ylabel('Velocity (m/s)')
ax2.set_title('Velocity vs Time')
ax2.legend()
ax2.axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.5)
 
plt.suptitle('Figure 7: Motion for different density models')
Out[17]:
Text(0.5, 0.98, 'Figure 7: Motion for different density models')
In [18]:
#part 4
for n in ns:
    sol = solutions_n[n]
    center_idx = np.argmin(np.abs(sol.y[0] + R_e))
    t_center = sol.t[center_idx]
    v_center = abs(sol.y[1][center_idx])
    print(f"n={n}: Time to center = {t_center:.1f} s, Speed = {v_center:.1f} m/s")

n=0: Time to center = 1266.2 s, Speed = 7876.7 m/s
n=1: Time to center = 1095.1 s, Speed = 10510.1 m/s
n=2: Time to center = 1034.2 s, Speed = 12080.6 m/s
n=9: Time to center = 943.0 s, Speed = 18398.4 m/s

Part 6: A Lunar Mine Shaft¶

Finally, with all of the framework in place, consider the case where the the mine shaft is instead dug on the moon.

  1. Compute the travel time to the center of the moon in case we dig a pole-to-pole mine shaft, so that we can again avoid the Coriolis force, though on the moon this force is very small. No drag force either, which is actually realistic for the moon!
  2. What is the density (if you asssume a constant density) of the moon compared to that of earth?
  3. How does the fall/orbit time depend on density? See if you can discover a relationship between the density and the fall time. You should think beyond strictly linear relationships.

This problem can actually be done on paper, and then you can compare the answer with that for the homogenous earth you did in Part 4.

In [19]:
g_moon = G * M_m / R_m**2
print(f"Surface gravity on Moon: {g_moon:.3f} m/s²")

#define the moon motion equationn
def motion_moon(t, state):
    y, v = state
    r = R_m + y
    g_r = g_moon * r / R_m  # linear decrease (uniform density)
    dydt = v
    dvdt = -g_r
    return [dydt, dvdt]
 
T_moon = 2 * np.pi * np.sqrt(R_m / g_moon)
t_span_moon = (0, T_moon)
t_eval_moon = np.linspace(0, T_moon, 2000)
 
sol_moon = solve_ivp(motion_moon, t_span_moon, init0,
                     t_eval=t_eval_moon, dense_output=True)
 
#find time to center
center_idx_moon = np.argmin(np.abs(sol_moon.y[0] + R_m))
t_center_moon = sol_moon.t[center_idx_moon]
 
print(f"Time to center of Moon: {t_center_moon:.1f} s = {t_center_moon/60:.1f} min")
print(f"Time to center of Earth: {T_analytical/4:.1f} s = {T_analytical/4/60:.1f} min")

Surface gravity on Moon: 1.624 m/s²
Time to center of Moon: 4874.5 s = 81.2 min
Time to center of Earth: 1266.8 s = 21.1 min
In [20]:
rho_earth = M_e / (4/3 * np.pi * R_e**3)
rho_moon = M_m / (4/3 * np.pi * R_m**3)
 
print(f"Earth density: {rho_earth:.0f} kg/m³")
print(f"Moon density: {rho_moon:.0f} kg/m³")
print(f"Ratio (Moon/Earth): {rho_moon/rho_earth:.3f}")

Earth density: 5495 kg/m³
Moon density: 3342 kg/m³
Ratio (Moon/Earth): 0.608

The density of the moon is about 60% of that of the earth (assuming a constant density throughout)

In [21]:
#for uniform density sphere: T = 2*pi*sqrt(R/g) = 2*pi*sqrt(R/(GM/R^2))
#                            T = 2*pi*sqrt(R^3/GM)
#but M = (4/3)*pi*R^3*rho, so GM = G*(4/3)*pi*R^3*rho
#therefore: T = 2*pi*sqrt(R^3 / (G*(4/3)*pi*R^3*rho))
#           T = 2*pi*sqrt(1 / (G*(4/3)*pi*rho))
#           T = sqrt(3*pi / (G*rho))
# So T is proportional to 1/sqrt(rho)
 
T_earth_predicted = np.sqrt(3*np.pi / (G*rho_earth))
T_moon_predicted = np.sqrt(3*np.pi / (G*rho_moon))
 
print(f"Earth period (analytical): {T_earth_predicted:.1f} s")
print(f"Moon period (analytical): {T_moon_predicted:.1f} s")
print(f"Fall time proportional to 1/sqrt(density)")
print(f"Ratio of times (Moon/Earth): {T_moon_predicted/T_earth_predicted:.3f}")
print(f"Ratio of 1/sqrt(densities): {np.sqrt(rho_earth/rho_moon):.3f}")
print("They match")

Earth period (analytical): 5069.4 s
Moon period (analytical): 6500.5 s
Fall time proportional to 1/sqrt(density)
Ratio of times (Moon/Earth): 1.282
Ratio of 1/sqrt(densities): 1.282
They match

Part 3¶

for uniform density sphere: $$T = 2\pi\sqrt{R/g} = 2\pi\sqrt{R/(GM/R^2)}$$ $$T = 2\pi\sqrt{R^3/GM}$$ but $M = (4/3)\pi R^3\rho$, so $$GM = G(4/3)\pi R^3\rho$$ therefore $$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{R^3}{G(4/3)\pi R^3\rho}}$$ $$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{1}{G(4/3)\pi\rho}}$$ $$T = \sqrt{\frac{3\pi}{G\rho}}$$

So T should be proportional to $1/\sqrt{rho}$, which will be checked below:¶

In [22]:
T_earth_predicted = np.sqrt(3*np.pi / (G*rho_earth))
T_moon_predicted = np.sqrt(3*np.pi / (G*rho_moon))
 
print(f"Earth period (analytical): {T_earth_predicted:.1f} s")
print(f"Moon period (analytical): {T_moon_predicted:.1f} s")
print(f"Fall time proportional to 1/sqrt(density)")
print(f"Ratio of times (Moon/Earth): {T_moon_predicted/T_earth_predicted:.3f}")
print(f"Ratio of 1/sqrt(densities): {np.sqrt(rho_earth/rho_moon):.3f}")
if np.isclose(np.sqrt(rho_earth/rho_moon), T_moon_predicted/T_earth_predicted):
    print(f'\nThe ratios match')

Earth period (analytical): 5069.4 s
Moon period (analytical): 6500.5 s
Fall time proportional to 1/sqrt(density)
Ratio of times (Moon/Earth): 1.282
Ratio of 1/sqrt(densities): 1.282

The ratios match

Part 7: Fourier Transforming Orbits¶

Take the solutions for n=0 and n=9 of the orbits in Part 5 (the inhomogeneous earth) and Fourier Transform them, to see what frequencies (1/periods) are dominant in the power spectra. Comment on what you see.

Hint: you could take the orbit from the previous integration in Part 5c, but it is useful to increase the integration time and sampling to get more meaningful smooth enough curves. We discussed this in class.

For an example of a spectral analysis of galactic orbits see https://articles.adsabs.harvard.edu/pdf/1982ApJ...252..308B

In [23]:
#need longer integration and finer sampling for good FFT
t_span_fft = (0, 10 * T_analytical)
n_points = 10000
t_eval_fft = np.linspace(t_span_fft[0], t_span_fft[1], n_points)
 
#integrate for n=0 and n=9
solutions_fft = {}
for n in [0,9]:
    sol = solve_ivp(lambda t, y: motion_nonuniform(t, y, n, rho_n_values[n]), t_span_fft, init0, t_eval=t_eval_fft, dense_output=True)
    solutions_fft[n] = sol
 
#perform FFT and plot
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(14, 10))
 
for i, n in enumerate([0, 9]):
    sol = solutions_fft[n]
    
    #time series
    axes[i,0].plot(sol.t, sol.y[0]/1000)
    axes[i,0].set_xlabel('Time (s)')
    axes[i,0].set_ylabel('Position (km)')
    axes[i,0].set_title(f'Time Series (n={n})')
    
    #FFT
    y_data = sol.y[0]
    dt = sol.t[1] - sol.t[0]
    
    yf = fft(y_data)
    xf = fftfreq(n_points, dt)
    
    #only positive frequencies
    pos_mask = xf > 0
    xf_pos = xf[pos_mask]
    yf_pos = np.abs(yf[pos_mask])
    power = yf_pos**2
    
    axes[i,1].plot(xf_pos, power)
    axes[i,1].set_xlabel('Frequency (Hz)')
    axes[i,1].set_ylabel('Power')
    axes[i,1].set_title(f'Power Spectrum (n={n})')
    axes[i,1].set_xlim(0, 0.0005)
    
    #find dominant frequency
    max_idx = np.argmax(power)
    dominant_freq = xf_pos[max_idx]
    dominant_period = 1 / dominant_freq
    
    print(f"n={n}:")
    print(f" Dominant frequency: {dominant_freq:.6e} Hz")
    print(f" Dominant period: {dominant_period:.1f} s = {dominant_period/60:.1f} min\n")
    
    axes[i,1].axvline(dominant_freq, color='red', linestyle='--', label=f'f={dominant_freq:.2e} Hz')
    axes[i,1].legend()

n=0:
 Dominant frequency: 1.973292e-05 Hz
 Dominant period: 50676.7 s = 844.6 min

n=9:
 Dominant frequency: 1.973292e-05 Hz
 Dominant period: 50676.7 s = 844.6 min

Describe what we see here:¶

The fourier analysis shows how both of our density models, uniform ($n=0$) and concentrated-core ($n=9$), exhibit spectra dominated by a single sharp peak at the fundamental annd lack any strong higher frequencies. Both have the same fundamental, and their shared lack of any harmonics indicated that the way the orbital periods behave is very similar.
